text: So no need to worry, we have just learned how to survive from the deepest well of troubles, by simply sweeping those troubles of your back and move one step up.
correct: So there is no need to worry. One can learn to survive the deepest well , by simply sweeping troubles from one's back and stepping to the surface. This requires two sentences. The first part is an independent clause and so is the second. A semi-colon would work, but it is safer with a period and two sentences. "deepest well" is a good metaphor, so "of troubles" isn't needed. "Move one step up" is not often used. "Stepping to the surface" completes the well metaphor and makes more sense.

text: Every time something bad happened to the king; the minister said,"Perhaps this is good." One day the king's finger was cut off, then the minister said, "Perhaps this is good." The king got very angry and said, "What's good about that?" Then he ordered that this minister be prisoned.
correct: Anytime something bad would happen to the king, the minister would say,"Perhaps this is good." One day, the king's finger was cut off and the minister said, "Perhaps this is good." The king got very angry and asked, "What's good about that?" Then he ordered that this minister be imprisoned. Often times past tense actions that are repeated over and over, get the auxiliary "would" before the verb. In this case, something bad is happening over and over. And also minister is saying something over and over. This is like "When I was young, I would climb trees." The semi-colon can't be used here, because it is not splitting two independent clauses. It just needs a comma. Introducing the time like "one day" always requires a comma after it. Also, "and" is always more safe to use in a sentence than ", then". Imprisoned is of course the verb for being thrown in prison.

text: He drifted away from his guards trying to follow his prey.
correct: He drifted away from his guards, trying to find a good fishing spot. "prey" isn't used by fishermen generally. They don't see it like hunting deer or bears or something like that (hunters might use "prey", but I'm not sure). I can't think of any better word to use here. Generally "follow" isn't used either, unless they have good technology; most fishermen don't. They will call it a fishing spot and roam around looking at different spots.

text: But when they realized that his finger was cut off, they couldn't sacrifice him because their sacrifice should be a perfect complete body.
correct: But when they realized that his finger was cut off, they couldn't sacrifice him because their sacrifice had to be a perfectly complete body. To modify "complete", you must use the adverb form of "perfect" which is "perfectly." Adverbs usually end in "-ly" and come before adjectives to modify them.

text: Some other people saw it and started talking about negligence of the people in charge, and that the king isn't doing his job by checking on those things.
correct: Some other people saw it and started talking about the negligence of people in charge, and many claimed that the king wasn't doing his job. I added "many claimed" in order to make the second part (after the ", and") an independent clause... with a subject and verb. I crossed out by checking on those things, because it is implied in "negligence" and that his job is to do such things.

text: At first I thought that this girl is Angelina Julie (when she was little), but then I thought, the difference between Angelina and Jim can't be that big.
correct: At first, I thought that this girl was a younger Angelina Jolie, but then I thought that the age difference between Jolie and Jim Carrey can't be that big. "Little" is sometimes used to mean younger. But usually you'd just use "younger". For instance, you'd say "When I was little, I used to play with trains." It'd mean younger, but ordinarily you'd say younger. "Little" has the implication of being young enough to be physically quite small. People would not refer to themselves past the age of like 14 as "little". Also, if you say Jolie's name twice, usually you'd say the last name the second time. You could even use "Carrey" here, but ordinarily you'd use the last name rather than first.

text: Let him come in and fill our home with fortune." She disagreed saying, "My dear, why don't we invite Success?"

text: I just hope that I don't loose my mind by the end of the semester.

Many people use them incorrectly even as English native-speakers, but it does not take long to know the difference after writing them a few times.

text: The participants only have 1 euro a day, it actually means they ain't got no money (or ain't no money?
correct: The participants only have 1 euro a day, it actually means they ain't got no money (or ain't no money? "Ain't" is mostly used in the south-east US (often just called the South), so it sounds odd from anyone who isn't from there (even me, from Boston!). "Actually" has to mean that something is true. If they have 1 euro per day, it isn't true that they don't have any money. They have 1 euro! You can say, "In effect, it means that they have no money" or "They have almost/practically no money." Double-negatives (are not = ain't.... no) are not standard English, and only used in certain dialects like Southern US English.

In English, when using an adjective to compare something else with something else (like saying that it is easier for you to speak up because you're an outgoing person vs. if you weren't an outgoing person), you use either "more or less + adjective" or "modified adjective + -er", depending on the word. In this case, easy has to be modified and have an -er added. When it's among 3+ things, then it's either "most or least + adjective" or "modified adjective + est". Most adjectives aren't modified, like harder, faster, slower, softer, etc. There are also irregular words, but that's too much for right now. I think it is just the words that end in -y, but I can't think of all the examples right now. text: They serve all dishes by only 130 yen at lunch time from Monday through Saturday, and marine ingredients are all fresh because they are caught in the sea around every morning(I heard the restaurant is run by a fisher).

The first just sounds a bit more natural, what you wrote is not bad though. I don't hear "marine ingredients" used for food ever, so I'd use "seafood". "caught from the sea" (we normally say "from" because you're describing the fish's origins, not the actual action of catching fish) sounds a bit odd so I'd simply say "caught fresh". "morning" is already a vague time period so we don't normally put "around". Finally, while some people might say "fisher", "fisherman" is both more common and appropriate.

"the" sounds odd, since it makes it sound like there is only supposed to be one alcohol made from sweet potato in the universe, and shochu is that alcohol. You can put "a" or simply say "alcohol".

(1) Adding "have" to "I had to..." means that this has happened many times, and is likely to happen again. If this has stopped happening, then "I had to..." would be correct. Because I changed it to "I had to...", I needed to use "closes", to show that it is still happening in the present.

text: The author attributes one of the reasons that causes anorexia to the TV industry because the industry has been set skinny models as the standard for beauty.
correct: The author one of the causes anorexia to the TV industry because has set skinny models as the standard for beauty. <"reason" and "cause" can in some cases be synonyms, so putting the two of them one after the other sounds redundant. "Cause" is not just a verb but also a noun which means pretty much what you want to convey with "reasons". Moreover, "reason" is a word that is usually associated with intentional human-action, while "cause" can mean something unintentional (even if done by humans). Because the TV industry didn't intentionally make people sick, it's better to use the word "cause" rather than "reason". It better conveys the sense of an unintentional side-effect. Also note that in English you must use commas (",") to separate between different sections of a long sentence.Another thing to note is that if you just mentioned the industry in the sentence, it's better to not repeat the word again right afterward, but instead to use the pronoun "IT". Because "set" is a used here in an active rather than passive form ("the industry HAS SET the standard"), you don't need the word "been" which would have been useful in the passive case ("the standard HAS BEEN set by the industry...")>

text: This year, the runners in the Hakone told me to keep working to accomplish what I really want to be.

If you say "working" without "hard" it sounds as if you're talking about physical labor or a job... Some alternatives: "keep working hard in order to accomplish" , "keep working hard so that I could accomplish", etc. But "working" like I said has some other connotations, so it might be better to use other words. For example "keep striving towards my goals", "to keep trying to fulfill my dreams", "to keep trying to become what I really want to be", "to keep aiming for my goals"... there's a million other options ^_^ >

text: My three-year-old daughter and two-year-old daughter made dolls for me.
correct: My three-year-old daughter and two-year-old daughter made dolls for me. [ Here's a tricky but worthwhile "shortcut" called the "suspended compound": "My three- and two-year-old daughters made dolls for me." An easier example: "All 4th- and 5th-grade students are to report to the auditorium at 13:30." "Compound" because you have two elements. "The first hyphen is suspended (like an unfinished bridge, attached at one side but not the other) intentionally to show that it's not related to the word next to it -- "and." When you read the sentence, the two hyphens placed where, and the way, they are, indicate that "4th" and "5th" are both (equally) "married" to the word "grade." See how that works? Your original sentence is OK, but it doesn't have to be that long and repetitive. I also added "," which sort of complicates things (because of the numbers that immediately follow), yet it tells us that you have two daughters -- one three-year-old daughter and one two-year-old daughter. Of course, you could always write: "My two daughters, ages 3 and 2, made me flowers." and not have to bother with, figure out, my not-so-little "lecture." ;-) ]

(When you have lists of actions ("take time" and "make corrections") I think it's best to make sure that your verbs are "parallel". That is, "taking" and "make" have different forms, but "take" and "make" have similar forms. Similarly, "taking" and "making" are similar and would sound good in a list (i.e. "She is taking her time and making a list."), but "take" and "making" are different and would sound a little strange in a list. However, what you had sounded pretty natural to me--I had to think for a while before giving this suggestion!)

When you use "the," it means that both you and the listener know who "the" refers to.

text: One of members' going to go to Canada this month.

Actually this is a subcategory contained within 'False Dilemna' logical fallacy, in which only two choices are considered when in fact there may be many options. I don't believe it is necessarily the standard thought process of an English speaker, and I'm sure some Japanese are guilty of thinking this way as well. Nonetheless, people with strong opinions, in my view, often use this illogical way of thinking. I also believe that there is a tendency for these people's strong opinions to be overrepresented, especially in settings such as internet message boards, etc., where people's normal inhibitions are often all but ignored. http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/False_dilemma

text: My 2 close co-friends will leave from the office after 2 days.

(3) The expression "will be leaving" focusses on the process of leaving and saying goodbye to everyone. It makes the action stretch out over time. The expression "will leave" concentrates on one moment: before they are here, after they are gone.

text: I should find something out for their left gift.

text: I have to go out of the office right now!

To "leave the office" focusses on what will happen afterwards - for example, going shopping to buy a present. Someone who has "gone out" might come back very soon. Someone who has "left" is not expected back until the next day.)

text: Try to control cell phones and do not let them control you and affect your life in away that causes you pai and suffering.

Don't let sentences be too complicated. And also, avoid talking directly about the reader or saying "you". It sounds too personal. It is better to talk about people in general. Another alternative is using "one" in place of "you", which is a bit more advanced. And something that is seen in English essays. For instance, "one should always control their cellphone use." Instead of "you should always..." Or "One's favorite art depends on their experience." instead of "Your favorite art depends..." But this takes some time to learn and comes with practice in English essay writing.
